title: Differential distribution of NAA and NAAG in human brain as determined by quantitative localized proton MRS
abstract:
Differential Distribution of NAA and NAAG in
Human Brain as Determined by Quantitative
Localized Proton MRS
Petra J. W. Pouwelsw and Jens Frahm
Biomedizinische NMR Forschungs GmbH am Max-Planck-Institut f¨ur biophysikalische Chemie, Am Faßberg 11, D-37077
G¨ottingen, Germany
Quantitative proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy was performed in frontal, parietal and occipital white and
gray matter of young adults with use of a fully relaxed, short-echo time stimulated echo acquisition mode
localization sequence at 2.0 T. Separate concentrations of the neuronal compounds N-acetylaspartate (NAA) and
N-acetylaspartylglutamate (NAAG) were obtained by user-independent spectral analysis (LCModel). Except for
occipital gray matter in which an NAA concentration of 10.1±1.0 mM correlated with enhanced neuronal density
in visual cortex, NAA was found to be homogeneously distributed throughout cortical white and gray matter at
a concentration of 8.0–8.9 mM. NAAG concentrations of 1.5–2.7 mM were higher in white matter than levels of
0.6–1.5 mM found in gray matter, contributing up to 25% of total N-acetyl-containing compounds. The frontal to
parieto-occipital increase of both gray and white matter NAAG levels is also reﬂected in the distribution of total
NAA. © 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
NMR in Biomed. 10, 73–78 (1997)
No. of Figures: 5
No. of Tables: 1
No. of References: 30
Keywords: N-acetylaspartate; N-acetylaspartylglutamate; human brain; proton spectroscopy
Received 21 November 1996; revised 23 December 1996; accepted 6 January 1997
INTRODUCTION
The most prominent signal in proton magnetic resonance
(MR) spectra of the healthy brain comes from an acetyl
resonance at about 2.0 ppm that predominantly belongs to
N-acetylaspartate (NAA), but also includes a corresponding
group 
from 
the 
dipeptide 
N-acetylaspartylglutamate
(NAAG).1–3 Both NAA and NAAG are among the most
abundant metabolites in the central nervous system, where
they are found almost exclusively in neurons.4–9 Immuno-
histochemical studies of rat forebrain have shown that
generally they are not localized in the same nerve cells:
while NAAG immunoreactivity has been observed in
speciﬁc neuronal populations and scattered nonpyramidal
neurons, NAA immunoreactivity was more broadly distrib-
uted, appearing strongest in many pyramidal neurons.10
Besides these differences in cellular distribution, differences
in function have been described, although possible correla-
tions remain to be established. Apart from being each others
metabolic precursors, NAA is thought to act as an acetyl
donor and possibly as an osmoregulator of the nerve cell,
whereas NAAG may be involved in neurotransmis-
sion.1, 6, 10–12
Knowledge about the distribution of NAA and NAAG in
the brain may give further insight into their roles in normal
neurochemistry and also serve as a more speciﬁc indicator
for diseases affecting neuronal cell populations. For exam-
ple, a decrease of the total amount of N-acetyl-containing
compounds (tNAA), which is generally interpreted as
neuronal damage or loss, is observed in many diseases
ranging from brain tumors, acute and chronic stroke, to
demyelinating and neurodegenerative disorders. On the
other hand, tNAA is known to be increased in Canavan’s
disease, a childhood leukodystrophy characterized by a
deﬁciency of aspartoacylase responsible for NAA hydroly-
sis. So far, however, in vivo MRS studies have not attempted
to distinguish between NAA and NAAG and usually refer to
tNAA. The purpose of this study was to assess the
feasibility of separating NAA and NAAG by quantitative
single voxel proton MRS, and to examine their regional
distribution in the healthy human brain.
EXPERIMENTAL
Studies of 34 healthy volunteers (aged 18–39 years, mean
27±4 years) were conducted at 2.0 T (Siemens Magnetom
SP4000, Erlangen) using the standard imaging head
coil. Small volumes-of-interest (VOI) of 4.1–6.4 ml were
chosen for frontal, parietal and occipital white matter to
prevent partial volume contamination with gray matter (Fig.
1(A)–(C)). Gray matter was measured in frontal (8–12 ml),
parietal (8–18 ml) and occipital brain (8 ml) in paramedian
positions spanning 20 mm across the interhemispheric
ﬁssure (Fig. 1(D)–(F)).
Fully relaxed, short-echo time spectra were recorded with
a single voxel stimulated echo aquisition mode (STEAM)
localization sequence (TR/TE/TM=6000/20/30 ms, 64
accumulations) as described previously.13 Particular care
was taken to optimize magnetic ﬁeld homogeneity and
water suppression by localized adjustments. The spectro-
scopic raw data were corrected for residual eddy current
effects, and calibrated in proportion to the coil loading using
the transmitter reference amplitude.14, 15 Absolute metabolite
w Correspondence to: P. J. W. Pouwels.
Contract grant sponsor: European Union (Program Biomed II).
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
CCC 0952–3480/97/020073–06 $17.50
NMR IN BIOMEDICINE, VOL. 10, 73–78 (1997)
 concentrations were estimated by LCModel, a user-inde-
pendent 
ﬁtting 
routine 
that 
uses 
a 
library 
of
concentration-calibrated model spectra of all individual
metabolites.15, 16 These concentrations are expressed as
Figure 1. T1-weighted images (three-dimensional fast low-angle shot (FLASH), TR/TE=15/6 ms, ﬂip angle 20°, 4 mm
partitions) of representative locations selected for MRS of: (A)–(C) white matter, and (D–F) gray matter in frontal,
parietal and occipital brain. The coronal images in (B) and (C) represent the volumes in frontal and occipital white
matter, respectively. The transverse images in (E) and (F) further characterize the volumes in frontal and parietal,
and occipital gray mater, respectively.
P. J. W. POUWELS AND J. FRAHM

main_text:
Differential Distribution of NAA and NAAG in
Human Brain as Determined by Quantitative
Petra J. W. Pouwelsw and Jens Frahm
Biomedizinische NMR Forschungs GmbH am Max-Planck-Institut f¨ur biophysikalische Chemie, Am Faßberg 11, D-37077
Quantitative proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy was performed in frontal, parietal and occipital white and
gray matter of young adults with use of a fully relaxed, short-echo time stimulated echo acquisition mode
localization sequence at 2.0 T. Separate concentrations of the neuronal compounds N-acetylaspartate (NAA) and
N-acetylaspartylglutamate (NAAG) were obtained by user-independent spectral analysis (LCModel). Except for
occipital gray matter in which an NAA concentration of 10.1±1.0 mM correlated with enhanced neuronal density
in visual cortex, NAA was found to be homogeneously distributed throughout cortical white and gray matter at
a concentration of 8.0–8.9 mM. NAAG concentrations of 1.5–2.7 mM were higher in white matter than levels of
0.6–1.5 mM found in gray matter, contributing up to 25% of total N-acetyl-containing compounds. The frontal to
parieto-occipital increase of both gray and white matter NAAG levels is also reﬂected in the distribution of total
NAA. © 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

Keywords: N-acetylaspartate; N-acetylaspartylglutamate; human brain; proton spectroscopy

(MR) spectra of the healthy brain comes from an acetyl
resonance at about 2.0 ppm that predominantly belongs to
N-acetylaspartate (NAA), but also includes a corresponding
(NAAG).1–3 Both NAA and NAAG are among the most
abundant metabolites in the central nervous system, where
they are found almost exclusively in neurons.4–9 Immuno-
histochemical studies of rat forebrain have shown that
generally they are not localized in the same nerve cells:
while NAAG immunoreactivity has been observed in
speciﬁc neuronal populations and scattered nonpyramidal
neurons, NAA immunoreactivity was more broadly distrib-
uted, appearing strongest in many pyramidal neurons.10

in function have been described, although possible correla-
tions remain to be established. Apart from being each others
metabolic precursors, NAA is thought to act as an acetyl
donor and possibly as an osmoregulator of the nerve cell,
whereas NAAG may be involved in neurotransmis-
Knowledge about the distribution of NAA and NAAG in
the brain may give further insight into their roles in normal
neurochemistry and also serve as a more speciﬁc indicator
for diseases affecting neuronal cell populations. For exam-
ple, a decrease of the total amount of N-acetyl-containing
compounds (tNAA), which is generally interpreted as
neuronal damage or loss, is observed in many diseases
ranging from brain tumors, acute and chronic stroke, to
demyelinating and neurodegenerative disorders. On the
other hand, tNAA is known to be increased in Canavan’s
disease, a childhood leukodystrophy characterized by a
deﬁciency of aspartoacylase responsible for NAA hydroly-
sis. So far, however, in vivo MRS studies have not attempted
to distinguish between NAA and NAAG and usually refer to
tNAA. The purpose of this study was to assess the
feasibility of separating NAA and NAAG by quantitative
single voxel proton MRS, and to examine their regional
distribution in the healthy human brain.
Studies of 34 healthy volunteers (aged 18–39 years, mean
27±4 years) were conducted at 2.0 T (Siemens Magnetom
SP4000, Erlangen) using the standard imaging head
coil. Small volumes-of-interest (VOI) of 4.1–6.4 ml were
chosen for frontal, parietal and occipital white matter to
prevent partial volume contamination with gray matter (Fig.
1(A)–(C)). Gray matter was measured in frontal (8–12 ml),
parietal (8–18 ml) and occipital brain (8 ml) in paramedian
positions spanning 20 mm across the interhemispheric
Fully relaxed, short-echo time spectra were recorded with
a single voxel stimulated echo aquisition mode (STEAM)
localization sequence (TR/TE/TM=6000/20/30 ms, 64
accumulations) as described previously.13 Particular care
was taken to optimize magnetic ﬁeld homogeneity and
water suppression by localized adjustments. The spectro-
scopic raw data were corrected for residual eddy current
effects, and calibrated in proportion to the coil loading using
the transmitter reference amplitude.14, 15 Absolute metabolite

© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
CCC 0952–3480/97/020073–06 $17.50
concentrations were estimated by LCModel, a user-inde-
concentration-calibrated model spectra of all individual
metabolites.15, 16 These concentrations are expressed as
Figure 1. T1-weighted images (three-dimensional fast low-angle shot (FLASH), TR/TE=15/6 ms, ﬂip angle 20°, 4 mm
partitions) of representative locations selected for MRS of: (A)–(C) white matter, and (D–F) gray matter in frontal,
parietal and occipital brain. The coronal images in (B) and (C) represent the volumes in frontal and occipital white
matter, respectively. The transverse images in (E) and (F) further characterize the volumes in frontal and parietal,
and occipital gray mater, respectively.
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
mmol/(liter VOI), and are not corrected for cerebrospinal
ﬂuid (CSF) contributions and T2 relaxation effects. T1
saturation was ignored as all experiments were performed
under fully relaxed conditions using sufﬁciently long
repetition times TR. Because the model spectra were
measured with similar parameters as the in vivo spectra,
only differences in T2 relaxation between in vivo and in vitro
must be accounted for. At 2.0 T T2 relaxation times for both
the NAA and NAAG N-acetyl protons are approximately
370 ms in vivo and 800 ms in vitro, leading to a correction
factor of 1.03 at an echo time of TE=20 ms.2, 15 Potential
contributions from CSF are negligible in the small white
matter volumes of young healthy adults, but amount to
about 5–7% for gray matter determinations, leading to a
correction factor of 1.06.15, 17
Representative spectra of the investigated areas in white and
gray matter are shown in Fig. 2. Figure 3 demonstrates that
a spectroscopic distinction between NAA and NAAG is
possible because of a slight shift of their proton resonances
as detected in model spectra recorded at the same ﬁeld
strength and with similar parameters as the in vivo studies.
The chemical shift difference between the N-acetyl reso-
nances is 0.035 ppm, which corresponds to 3.0 Hz at 2.0 T.
Since the experimental spectra from human white and gray
matter have a mean linewidth of 3.5±0.4 Hz, this difference
is visible in spectra that are not ﬁltered before Fourier
transformation. Figure 4 shows scaled summations of such
unﬁltered spectra, with an enlargement of the N-acetyl
signal. A relatively large shoulder at 2.045 ppm can be
recognized in the parietal and occipital white matter spectra,
which is much smaller in frontal white matter, and hardly
visible in gray matter spectra.
Absolute metabolite concentrations of tNAA, NAA and
NAAG are summarized in Table 1, with the latter two
values also graphically depicted in Fig. 5. NAA is constant
throughout white and gray matter at a concentration of
8.0–8.9 mM. Only in occipital gray matter, which corre-
sponds to the functional area of the visual cortex, is the
NAA concentration slightly, but signiﬁcantly elevated in
comparison to other areas. In general, white mater contains
a considerable amount of NAAG, in accordance with the
qualitative observations in Fig. 4. The concentration of
2.7 mM in parietal and occipital regions is signiﬁcantly
higher than the 1.5 mM level in frontal white matter and
amounts to about 25% of tNAA. Gray matter NAAG
Figure 2. Proton MR spectra (STEAM, TR/TE/TM=6000/20/30 ms, 64 accumulations) of (left) frontal (5.1 ml), parietal (6.4 ml) and
occipital (5.1 ml) white matter (22-year-old volunteer), and (right) frontal (12 ml), parietal (18 ml) and occipital (8 ml) paramedian
gray matter (21-year-old volunteer). Additional metabolites indicated are creatine and phosphocreatine (Cr), choline-containing
compounds (Cho) and myo-inositol (Ins).
DISTRIBUTION OF NAA AND NAAG IN HUMAN BRAIN
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
concentrations are much lower than in white matter,
generally below 1 mM, with again a higher value of 1.5 mM
only found in occipital cortex. Both in gray and white
matter NAAG increases caudally, i.e. from frontal to
Of course, the spectroscopic differences between NAA
and NAAG are quite small, and quantiﬁcation of NAAG in
single spectra therefore strongly depends on the spectral
quality. However, intersubject averaging of a large number
of spectra gives a reliable regional distribution of NAAG,
despite relatively large standard deviations. In principle,
NAAG should be easily detected in spectra of children,
which are usually characterized by very small linewidths
presumably due to low concentrations of ions as well as
smaller head sizes and more spherical shapes. Preliminary
results indeed show that NAAG can be well determined in
white matter spectra of children, showing low concentra-
tions at birth and a slow increase, with adult values not
reached until adolescence. In this study of young adults the
observed distribution of NAAG is unlikely to be due to
regional differences in magnetic ﬁeld homogeneity. Mean
linewidths are shown in Table 1, and they do not differ
much between selected regions.
The present results are in line with a previous determina-
tion by Michaelis et al. reporting a 22% contribution of
NAAG to tNAA in parietal white matter.15 Most other in
vivo MRS studies of regional metabolite distributions in
human brain have not distinguished between NAA and
NAAG.18–22 However, published tNAA levels agree very
well with our results. For example, Hennig et al. measured
a higher concentration of tNAA in mixed gray and white
Figure 3. Proton MR spectra (STEAM, TR/TE/TM=10 000/
20/30 ms, 64–256 accumulations, 15 ml VOI) of model solutions
of NAA and NAAG using trimethylsilylpropanesulfonic acid
(TSPS) as chemical shift reference (0.0 ppm). Additional reso-
nances in the NAAG spectrum, in particular at 2.15 ppm, belong
to the methylene protons of the glutamate moiety.
Figure 4. Scaled summation of unﬁltered proton MR spectra of (left) frontal (n=22), parietal (n=20) and occipital (n=27) white
matter, and (right) frontal (n=12), parietal (n=19) and occipital (n=14) gray matter.
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
matter volumes in the occipital hemisphere (10.4±2.7 mM)
as compared to the parietal (8.2±2.2 mM) or frontal
hemisphere (8.4±1.6 mM).18 These data were conﬁrmed by
Christiansen et al., who determined 11.4±0.9 mM of tNAA
in the occipital and 10.0±1.3 mM in the frontal hemisphere
in mixed white and gray matter volumes of young
volunteers.19 Using long echo-time MRSI, both Tedeschi et
al. and Soher et al. showed that in white matter tNAA is
higher than in gray matter areas.21, 22 In an MRSI study by
Hetherington et al. quantitation was performed using image
segmentation, assuming a constant metabolite level within a
single tissue type.23 However, this approach is in contra-
diction with the heterogeneous distribution of tNAA
yielding a 25% higher concentration in occipital than in
The regional distribution of NAA in gray matter parallels
ﬁndings for neuronal cell density, which has been reported
to be constant in the neocortex with the exclusive exception
of being much higher in primary visual cortex (Brodmann’s
area 17).24, 25 In this area the ratio of neuronal to glial cell
density is higher than in surrounding areas, whereas the
total cellular density remains constant, which implies that
the total neuronal volume will also be larger.25 Since a part
of the occipital lobe consists of area 17, this probably
explains the higher MRS-detected NAA concentration in
this region. The correlation between NAA level and
neuronal density also lends further support to its use as a
general neuronal marker in proton MRS. Conversely,
however, this ﬁnding does not give an indication for a
functional relationship that is speciﬁc for a certain cortical
area. As far as NAAG is concerned, its distinctly higher
concentration in occipital gray matter may not only be due
to differences in cortical neuronal density, but also reﬂect a
more general caudal NAAG gradient reported to proceed
from cerebrum to spinal cord.6 The apparent discrepancy
between NAA being a neuronal compound and its high
concentration in white matter also holds true for NAAG.
Considering that white matter consists of myelinated axons,
the MRS data unambiguously indicate that NAA and
NAAG must be highly concentrated not only in cell bodies
and synapses, but also in axons. This has been conﬁrmed by
immunohistochemistry, which has demonstrated NAA and
NAAG immunoreactivity in all parts of the nerve cells,
including dendrites, axons and synaptic terminals.26
In summary, this study has shown that tNAA is
heterogeneously distributed in human brain and not constant
within a single tissue type. The observation of a caudal
concentration increase is mainly due to the underlying
increase of NAAG. NAA exhibits a homogeneous distribu-
tion throughout white and gray matter, with the only
exception of visual cortex. The correlation between NAA
and neuronal density supports the use of NAA as a neuronal
marker. Although NAAG is more concentrated in white than
in gray matter, little attention has been paid to its possible
Table 1. Absolute metabolite concentrations in white and gray matter of young
healthy adults. (Top) Values are given in mmol/(liter VOI)±SD without
corrections for CSF partial volume and differential T2 relaxation.
(Bottom) Values are given in mM±SD including corrections for CSF
partial volume and differential T2 relaxation (see Experimental)
Statistical signiﬁcance (two-sided t-test) is indicated for comparisons with parietal
white or gray matter, respectively (***p<0.001, **p<0.01).
a Mean of full linewidth (Hz) at half height of the Cr signal at 3.03 ppm.
Figure 5. Absolute concentrations (mM±SD) of NAA and NAAG in white and gray
matter. Statistical signiﬁcance (two-sided t-test) is indicated for a comparison with
parietal white or gray matter, respectively (***p<0.001, **p<0.01).
DISTRIBUTION OF NAA AND NAAG IN HUMAN BRAIN
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
function in white matter or in diseased brain. In only a few
cases, NAAG abnormalities in high-performance liquid
chromatography (HPLC) assays of autopsied gray matter
have tentatively been attributed to a dysfunction of
excitatory neurotransmission.27–30 A preliminary retrospec-
tive assessment of our patient MRS data base revealed a
parallel decrease of NAAG and NAA in disorders asso-
ciated with neurodegeneration. Future MRS as well as
immunohistochemical studies of a wider range of patholo-
gies may provide for a better understanding of the functions
